reasoning: The EFFR (Effective Federal Funds Rate) is directly tied to the target range set by the FOMC. In the January 27-28, 2026 meeting, the FOMC opted to hold the target range steady at 3.50% - 3.75%. Currently, the EFFR has remained precisely flat at 3.64% since mid-December 2025, showing zero day-to-day fluctuation. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for March 17-18, 2026, which means no planned changes to the target rate will occur before both resolution dates (Feb 8 and March 3). Historically over the last several years, the EFFR rarely fluctuates by even 1 basis point outside of target range changes or severe overnight repo stress. Because it is highly likely the rate continues to remain completely flat at 3.64% through early March, the probability of the EFFR strictly increasing relative to its Feb 1 value is exceptionally low. I am setting the probabilities at the minimum allowable boundary (0.05/0.06) to account for completely unforeseen inter-meeting moves, while maintaining the required increasing uncertainty with the forecast horizon.
